name: What’s the difference between a prehung door and a slab door, and which should I use?
headline: What’s the difference between a prehung door and a slab door, and which should I use?

mainEntity.name: What’s the difference between a prehung door and a slab door, and which should I use?
mainEntity.text: What’s the difference between a prehung door and a slab door, and which should I use?

acceptedAnswer.text: A prehung door comes from the factory already mounted in its own frame, with hinges attached and usually a pre-cut hole for the lock. This makes installation easier because you replace the whole unit instead of hanging a new slab on an old frame. A slab door is just the door panel by itself, with no frame or hinges. Prehung doors are often the better choice when you’re doing a major replacement, especially if the existing frame is damaged, warped, or not square. They help ensure the door and frame line up correctly. For exterior doors, prehung units also make it simpler to get good weatherstripping and a proper threshold. Slab doors can be fine if your existing frame is in great shape and you just want to update the look. They’re often cheaper up front, but they require more skill to mortise hinges, cut the lockset holes, and align everything. Pitfalls include buying a slab door when your frame is already twisted or rotted; you’ll fight with the fit and still end up with a poor seal. Another common issue is ordering the wrong size. Always measure your existing door and opening carefully, and pay attention to thickness, width, height, and swing direction. If you’re unsure or dealing with an exterior entry door, a prehung unit is usually safer and more forgiving.
